title: Maternity and Parental Leave etc. Regulations 1999
chapters: SI 1999/3312 · Made 10 December 1999 · In Force 15 December 1999 (Parts I–III) and 15 January 2000 (Part IV) · Made under ERA 1996 ss.71–80 and s.236 · Amended by the Maternity and Parental Leave etc. and the Paternity and Adoption Leave (Amendment) Regulations 2008 (SI 2008/1966), the Maternity and Parental Leave (Amendment) Regulations 2014 (SI 2014/3221), the Shared Parental Leave Regulations 2014 (SI 2014/3050), the Parental Leave (EU Directive) Regulations 2013 (SI 2013/283), and the Employment Rights Act 2025
desc: The Maternity and Parental Leave etc. Regulations 1999 implement the statutory framework for maternity leave, parental leave, and associated protections in employment. They are made under the enabling powers in Part VIII of the Employment Rights Act 1996 (ss.71–80). The Regulations define the entitlements to ordinary maternity leave (OML, 26 weeks) and additional maternity leave (AML, a further 26 weeks), totalling 52 weeks. They establish the right to return to work after maternity leave, protection from detriment and dismissal for reasons connected with pregnancy or maternity leave, and the right to unpaid parental leave (18 weeks per child, up to age 18). The Employment Rights Act 2025 introduces further amendments including enhanced protections during pregnancy and family leave, and strengthened redundancy protections for pregnant employees and those on maternity leave. Several ERA 2025 provisions affecting maternity rights are enacted but not yet in force pending commencement orders.

sections.title: Interpretation
sections.summary: Defines key terms used throughout the Regulations. 'Additional maternity leave period' means the period calculated in accordance with regulation 7. 'Childbirth' means the birth of a living child or the birth of a child after 24 weeks of pregnancy whether living or dead. 'Expected week of childbirth' means the week, beginning with midnight between Saturday and Sunday, in which it is expected that childbirth will occur. 'Ordinary maternity leave period' means 26 weeks beginning with the date on which the employee's OML period begins. 'Parental leave' means leave under regulation 13(1).

versions.description: Original definitions. Parental leave provisions came into force 15 January 2000.

sections.title: Entitlement to ordinary maternity leave
sections.summary: An employee is entitled to ordinary maternity leave (OML) provided she satisfies the notification requirements. There is no qualifying period of service — all employees are entitled to OML from day one of employment. The employee must notify her employer of her pregnancy, the expected week of childbirth, and the date she intends her OML to begin, no later than the end of the 15th week before the expected week of childbirth, or as soon as reasonably practicable.
sections.text:
(1) An employee is entitled to ordinary maternity leave provided that she satisfies the following conditions—
(a) no later than the end of the 15th week before her expected week of childbirth, or, if that is not reasonably practicable, as soon as is reasonably practicable, she notifies her employer of—
(i) her pregnancy;
(ii) the expected week of childbirth; and
(iii) the date on which she intends her ordinary maternity leave period to start;
and
(b) if requested to do so by her employer, she produces for his inspection a certificate from—
(i) a registered medical practitioner, or
(ii) a registered midwife,
stating the expected week of childbirth.

(1A) Where, by virtue of paragraph (1)(a)(iii), an employee's ordinary maternity leave period would begin before the beginning of the 11th week before the expected week of childbirth, the employee's ordinary maternity leave period shall instead begin on the first day of the 11th week before the expected week of childbirth.

(2) The notification under paragraph (1)(a) shall be given in writing, if the employer so requests.

(3) The date mentioned in paragraph (1)(a)(iii) (or, where applicable, paragraph (1A)), subject to paragraph (4), is the date on which the employee's ordinary maternity leave period starts.

(4) Where the employee is absent from work wholly or partly because of pregnancy on any day after the beginning of the 4th week before the expected week of childbirth, her ordinary maternity leave period starts on the day after that day.

versions.description: Original provision. Required 26 weeks' continuous employment for AML (OML was always day-one right). OML was 18 weeks.
OML extended from 18 to 26 weeks by the Maternity and Parental Leave (Amendment) Regulations 2002 (SI 2002/2789).

Maternity and Parental Leave etc. and the Paternity and Adoption Leave (Amendment) Regulations 2008 (SI 2008/1966) removed the 26-week qualifying period for AML — all maternity leave became a day-one right. Additional notification requirements clarified.

leadingCases.name: Alabaster v Woolwich plc [2005] ECJ Case C-147/02
leadingCases.heldText: A pay rise awarded during maternity leave must be reflected in the employee's statutory maternity pay calculation. The Pregnant Workers Directive requires that maternity pay be calculated by reference to current pay, not the pay at the time maternity leave commenced.
leadingCases.significance: Pay rises during maternity leave must be reflected in SMP — employers cannot freeze pay for SMP purposes.

refs: ERA 1996 s.72 (compulsory maternity leave — 2 weeks minimum)

sections.title: Duration of ordinary maternity leave
sections.summary: The ordinary maternity leave period is a period of 26 weeks beginning with the date on which OML starts. During OML, the employee is entitled to the benefit of all terms and conditions of employment which would have applied if she had not been absent, except remuneration. The employee is bound by any obligations arising under those terms and conditions.
sections.text:
(1) Subject to paragraph (2), an employee's ordinary maternity leave period continues for the period of 26 weeks from its commencement, or until the end of any later period in which she is entitled to leave by virtue of regulation 6A.

(2) Subject to paragraph (3), an employee's ordinary maternity leave period shall end in the circumstances set out in regulation 6B (end of maternity leave where employee is dismissed).

(3) Where an employee is entitled to ordinary maternity leave and additional maternity leave, her ordinary maternity leave period shall end at the end of the period of 26 weeks from its commencement, even if she is dismissed before the end of that period.

versions.description: Original provision: OML was 18 weeks.
OML extended to 26 weeks by SI 2002/2789.

leadingCases.name: Merino Gómez v Continental Industrias del Caucho SA [2004] ECJ Case C-342/01
leadingCases.heldText: Where maternity leave coincides with the annual leave period fixed for the entire workforce, the employee must be allowed to take her annual leave at a different time. The right to annual leave cannot be lost because of maternity leave.
leadingCases.significance: Annual leave cannot be lost due to overlap with maternity leave — must be rescheduled.

refs: reg.9 MPL 1999 (terms and conditions during OML)
ERA 1996 s.72 (compulsory maternity leave period — minimum 2 weeks)

sections.title: Entitlement to additional maternity leave
sections.summary: An employee who is entitled to ordinary maternity leave is also entitled to additional maternity leave. AML begins on the day after the last day of OML and continues for a further 26 weeks, giving a total entitlement of 52 weeks' maternity leave. Since the 2008 amendments, there is no qualifying period of continuous employment for AML — it is a day-one right.
sections.text:
(1) An employee who is entitled to ordinary maternity leave is also entitled to additional maternity leave.

(2) The additional maternity leave period commences on the day after the last day of the employee's ordinary maternity leave period.

(4) The additional maternity leave period of an employee entitled to such leave continues until the end of the period of 26 weeks from its commencement.

versions.description: Original provision: AML required 26 weeks' continuous employment at the 15th week before EWC. AML was 29 weeks.
AML extended to 26 weeks (from date OML ends, giving 52 weeks total).
26-week qualifying period for AML abolished. All employees entitled to AML from day one of employment.

leadingCases.name: O'Brien v Bolton St Catherine's Academy [2017] UKSC 16
leadingCases.heldText: The Supreme Court considered the interaction between maternity leave and redundancy selection. The employer cannot select an employee for redundancy because she is on maternity leave. However, the employer is not required to give the employee priority in redundancy selection merely because she is on AML.
leadingCases.significance: Distinguished between protection from dismissal because of maternity leave (protected) and priority in selection (not required during AML outside of reg.10 circumstances).

refs: Statutory Maternity Pay: SMP payable for 39 weeks (ERA 1996 does not govern SMP — see Social Security Contributions and Benefits Act 1992 ss.164–171)

sections.title: Terms and conditions during OML
sections.summary: During ordinary maternity leave, the employee is entitled to the benefit of all terms and conditions of employment which would have applied if she had not been absent, except terms and conditions about remuneration. 'Remuneration' means sums payable to an employee by way of wages or salary. The employee remains bound by her contractual obligations other than the obligation to work. This means benefits such as company car, gym membership, pension contributions (employer's share), and contractual holiday continue.
sections.text:
(1) An employee who takes ordinary maternity leave—
(a) is entitled, during the period of leave, to the benefit of all of the terms and conditions of employment which would have applied if she had not been absent;
(b) is bound, during that period, by any obligations arising under those terms and conditions, subject only to the qualification in section 71(4)(b) of the 1996 Act.

(2) In paragraph (1)(a), "terms and conditions of employment" has the meaning given by section 71(5) of the 1996 Act, and accordingly does not include terms and conditions about remuneration.

(3) For the purposes of section 71 of the 1996 Act, only sums payable to an employee by way of wages or salary are to be treated as remuneration.

versions.description: Original provision establishing the non-remuneration benefit continuation during OML.

leadingCases.name: GUS Home Shopping Ltd v Green & McLaughlin [2001] IRLR 75
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that an employee on OML is entitled to the benefit of all terms and conditions except remuneration. Contractual holiday accrues during maternity leave. The employer cannot require the employee to take annual leave during maternity leave.
leadingCases.significance: Confirmed that annual leave accrues during maternity leave and cannot be set off against maternity leave

refs: ERA 1996 s.71(4)–(5) (terms and conditions during OML — enabling provision)
reg.17 MPL 1999 (terms and conditions during AML — more limited rights than OML)
Working Time Regulations 1998 reg.13 (annual leave accrual — continues during maternity leave)

sections.title: Redundancy during OML
sections.summary: Where during an employee's OML period it is not practicable by reason of redundancy for the employer to continue to employ her under her existing contract, she is entitled to be offered a suitable available vacancy before the end of her employment under her existing contract. The new contract must not be substantially less favourable. This gives pregnant employees and those on maternity leave priority over other employees in redundancy situations. The Employment Rights Act 2025 strengthens this protection significantly.
sections.text:
(1) Where during an employee's ordinary maternity leave period it is not practicable by reason of redundancy for her employer to continue to employ her under her existing contract of employment, she is entitled to be offered (before the end of her employment under that contract) alternative employment with her employer or his successor, or an associated employer, under a new contract of employment which complies with paragraph (2).

(2) The new contract of employment must be such that—
(a) the work to be done under it is of a kind which is both suitable in relation to the employee and appropriate for her to do in the circumstances; and
(b) the provisions of the new contract as to the capacity and place in which she is to be employed, and as to the other terms and conditions of her employment, are not substantially less favourable to her than if she had continued to be employed under the previous contract.

versions.description: Original provision establishing priority right to suitable alternative vacancy during redundancy.

Protection from Redundancy (Pregnancy and Family Leave) Act 2023 extended the period of enhanced redundancy protection from the maternity leave period itself to include the period of pregnancy before leave and 18 months after the expected week of childbirth (or date of birth). Implemented through amendments to MPL 1999.

pendingAmendment.effect: ERA 2025 further strengthens redundancy protections during pregnancy and family leave. Specific provisions include enhanced consultation requirements and extended protection periods. Details subject to commencement orders and potential secondary legislation.
pendingAmendment.commencementStatus: Enacted. Not yet in force. Subject to commencement orders under ERA 2025.
pendingAmendment.anticipatedDate: TBC — subject to Secretary of State commencement order
pendingAmendment.aceiImpact: Family Leave category. Enhanced redundancy protection during pregnancy/maternity will affect employer exposure calculations in Dismissal and Family Leave ACEI categories.

sections.eraNote: ERA 2025 contains provisions that further strengthen redundancy protections for pregnant employees and those on family leave. These provisions are enacted but not yet in force. The Protection from Redundancy (Pregnancy and Family Leave) Act 2023 already extended protection; ERA 2025 builds on this foundation. Forward Intelligence classification: HIGH (Royal Assent achieved).

leadingCases.name: Sefton Borough Council v Wainwright [2015] UKEAT/0168/14
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT confirmed that the right to be offered a suitable available vacancy in a redundancy situation is a positive obligation on the employer. The employer must offer the vacancy without requiring the employee to apply or compete for it. Failure to do so renders the dismissal automatically unfair.
leadingCases.significance: Confirmed that the suitable alternative vacancy right is a positive offer obligation, not a right to compete

refs: ERA 1996 s.99 (unfair dismissal — pregnancy/maternity — automatically unfair)
ERA 1996 s.108(3)(b) (no qualifying period for pregnancy/maternity dismissal)
Protection from Redundancy (Pregnancy and Family Leave) Act 2023
Employment Rights Act 2025 (further enhanced protections — not yet in force)

sections.title: Right to return after maternity leave
sections.summary: An employee who returns to work after OML is entitled to return to the job in which she was employed before her absence. An employee who returns after AML is entitled to return to the job in which she was employed before her absence, or if it is not reasonably practicable, to another job which is both suitable and appropriate for her in the circumstances. 'Job' means the nature of the work, the capacity and place of employment. The right to return is on terms and conditions not less favourable than would have applied had she not been absent, with seniority, pension rights and similar rights as if continuous.
sections.text:
(1) An employee who returns to work after ordinary maternity leave is entitled to return to the job in which she was employed before her absence.

(2) An employee who returns to work after additional maternity leave, or after a period of leave which included additional maternity leave, is entitled to return to the job in which she was employed before her absence, or, if it is not reasonably practicable for the employer to permit her to return to that job, to another job which is both suitable for her and appropriate for her to do in the circumstances.

(3) The right to return under paragraph (1) or (2) is a right to return—
(a) with her seniority, pension rights and similar rights as they would have been if the period, or periods, of her employment prior to her additional maternity leave period (or last such period) and her employment on return were continuous;
(b) on terms and conditions not less favourable than those which would have applied if she had not been absent.

versions.description: Original provision. Right to return to same job after OML; same or suitable alternative after AML.
SI 2008/1966 aligned the right to return provisions with the removal of the AML qualifying period. Return rights after OML and AML given equivalent status in principle.

leadingCases.name: Blundell v The Governing Body of St Andrew's Catholic Primary School [2007] ICR 1451
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that the right to return after AML to a job which is 'suitable for her and appropriate for her to do in the circumstances' requires the employer to give proper consideration to the employee's individual circumstances, including her childcare arrangements and any changes to the workplace during her absence.
leadingCases.significance: Clarified the scope of 'suitable and appropriate' alternative job on return from AML

refs: ERA 1996 s.71(4) (terms during OML — enabling provision)
ERA 1996 s.73(4) (terms during AML — enabling provision)

sections.title: Protection from detriment
sections.summary: An employee is entitled not to be subjected to any detriment by any act or deliberate failure to act by her employer for prescribed reasons connected with pregnancy, maternity leave, or parental leave. Prescribed reasons include pregnancy itself, taking maternity leave, taking parental leave, declining to sign a workforce agreement relating to parental leave, and being redundant and not being offered a suitable vacancy in circumstances where regulation 10 applied.

versions.description: Original provision. Prescribed detriment reasons connected with pregnancy, maternity leave, and parental leave.
SI 2006/2014 expanded prescribed reasons to include kept-in-touch days during maternity leave.
Shared Parental Leave Regulations 2014 added shared parental leave to the prescribed reasons.

leadingCases.name: New Southern Railway Ltd v Quinn [2006] ICR 761
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that a failure to carry out a risk assessment for a pregnant employee under MHSWR 1999 reg.16 can amount to detriment under reg.19 of the MPL 1999, because the employee is denied the protection that the assessment is intended to provide.
leadingCases.significance: Failure to risk-assess a pregnant employee is detriment under reg.19.

refs: ERA 1996 s.47C (right not to suffer detriment in relation to pregnancy, maternity, parental leave)

sections.title: Unfair dismissal
sections.summary: An employee who is dismissed shall be regarded as unfairly dismissed if the reason or principal reason for the dismissal is connected with pregnancy, childbirth, maternity leave, parental leave, or time off for dependants. This is automatically unfair dismissal — no qualifying period of continuous employment is required and the employee does not need to show the dismissal was unreasonable under s.98(4). Pregnancy-related dismissals are also likely to constitute direct sex discrimination under section 18 of the Equality Act 2010.

versions.description: Original provision establishing automatically unfair dismissal for pregnancy/maternity/parental leave reasons.
Maternity and Parental Leave etc. (Amendment) Regulations 2006 (SI 2006/2014) expanded prescribed reasons to include kept-in-touch days during maternity leave.

leadingCases.name: Caledonia Bureau Investment & Property v Caffrey [1998] ICR 603
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that a dismissal related to pregnancy includes a dismissal for pregnancy-related illness. Where an employee is dismissed for illness that is causally linked to pregnancy, the dismissal is automatically unfair.
leadingCases.significance: Established that pregnancy-related illness dismissal is automatically unfair

leadingCases.name: Brown v Rentokil Ltd [1998] ECJ Case C-394/96
leadingCases.heldText: The ECJ held that dismissal of a female worker at any time during her pregnancy for absences due to incapacity caused by illness resulting from that pregnancy is direct sex discrimination. The protective period extends throughout the period of pregnancy.
leadingCases.significance: ECJ authority confirming pregnancy-related illness dismissal as direct sex discrimination

leadingCases.name: Atkins v Coyle Personnel plc [2008] IRLR 420
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that where the employer fails to offer a suitable available vacancy to an employee on maternity leave in a redundancy situation (contrary to regulation 10), the dismissal is automatically unfair under regulation 20. The obligation is absolute — no balancing exercise is required.
leadingCases.significance: Confirmed the absolute nature of the suitable alternative vacancy obligation during maternity redundancy

refs: ERA 1996 s.99 (unfair dismissal — pregnancy/maternity — enabling provision)
ERA 1996 s.108(3)(b) (no qualifying period for s.99 dismissal)
ERA 1996 s.124(1A) (no cap on compensation for s.99 dismissal)

sections.title: Entitlement to parental leave
sections.summary: An employee who has been continuously employed for a period of not less than one year and who has, or expects to have, responsibility for a child is entitled to be absent from work on parental leave for the purpose of caring for that child. Entitlement is 18 weeks per child, up to the child's 18th birthday. The entitlement was increased from 13 to 18 weeks by the Parental Leave (EU Directive) Regulations 2013. Parental leave is unpaid.
sections.text:
(1) An employee who—
(a) has been continuously employed for a period of not less than one year; and
(b) has, or expects to have, responsibility for a child,
is entitled, in accordance with these Regulations, to be absent from work on parental leave for the purpose of caring for that child.

(1A) An employee has responsibility for a child, for the purposes of paragraph (1), if—
(a) he or she has parental responsibility for the child; or
(b) he or she has been registered as the child's father under any provision of the Births and Deaths Registration Act 1953 or the Births and Deaths Registration Act (Northern Ireland) 1976.

(2) An employee's entitlement to parental leave under this regulation is to a total of 18 weeks' leave in respect of any individual child.

versions.description: Original provision. 13 weeks per child. Available until child's 5th birthday (or 5 years from placement for adopted children, or 18th birthday for disabled children).
Parental Leave (EU Directive) Regulations 2013 (SI 2013/283) increased entitlement from 13 to 18 weeks per child and extended the age limit from 5 to 18 for all children.

leadingCases.name: Rodway v New Southern Railway Ltd [2005] EWCA Civ 443
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that parental leave is a right to unpaid leave for the purpose of caring for a child. The employer cannot unreasonably postpone parental leave where the employee has given proper notice. However, the employer may postpone if the business would be unduly disrupted.
leadingCases.significance: Clarified the employer's limited right to postpone parental leave requests.

refs: ERA 1996 s.76 (entitlement to parental leave — enabling provision)
reg.14 MPL 1999 (extent of entitlement — 18 weeks per child)
Schedule 2 MPL 1999 (default parental leave provisions — blocks of 1 week, 4 weeks per year per child, 21 days notice)

sections.title: Extent of entitlement
sections.summary: An employee's entitlement to parental leave is a total of 18 weeks in respect of any individual child. Leave is taken in blocks of one week (under the default scheme in Schedule 2), except for parents of disabled children who may take leave in individual days. A maximum of 4 weeks' parental leave may be taken in respect of any individual child in any one year. 21 days' notice is required.
sections.text:
(1) An employee's entitlement to parental leave under regulation 13 is to a total of 18 weeks' leave in respect of any individual child.

(2) Except in a case where a relevant agreement makes provision which differs from this paragraph, an employee may not exercise any entitlement to parental leave in respect of a child after the date of the child's eighteenth birthday.

versions.description: Original: 13 weeks. Age limit: 5th birthday (18 for disabled children).
Increased to 18 weeks. Age limit extended to 18th birthday for all children.

pendingAmendments: ERA 2025 — further strengthened redundancy protections during pregnancy/family leave (enacted, not yet in force)
ERA 2025 — enhanced maternity protections during initial employment period (enacted, not yet in force)
Protection from Redundancy (Pregnancy and Family Leave) Act 2023 — 18-month post-birth protection IN FORCE from April 2024
Post-Brexit — MPL 1999 now assimilated law. Pregnant Workers Directive 92/85/EEC retained as assimilated law under REUL Act 2023

parliamentaryOrigin.enablingAct: Employment Rights Act 1996 ss.71–80 and s.236 (as inserted/amended by Employment Relations Act 1999)
parliamentaryOrigin.euDirective: Pregnant Workers Directive 92/85/EEC and Parental Leave Directive 96/34/EC (now replaced by Work-Life Balance Directive 2019/1158)
parliamentaryOrigin.madeBy: Secretary of State for Trade and Industry
parliamentaryOrigin.note: The MPL 1999 Regulations were made under the enabling powers inserted into ERA 1996 by the Employment Relations Act 1999. They implemented both the Pregnant Workers Directive 92/85/EEC (maternity leave framework) and the Parental Leave Directive 96/34/EC (unpaid parental leave). The original OML was 18 weeks, extended to 26 weeks by SI 2002/2789. AML originally required 26 weeks' continuous employment — this qualifying period was abolished by SI 2008/1966. The Regulations have been substantially amended: SI 2002/2789, SI 2006/2014, SI 2008/1966, SI 2013/283, SI 2014/3221. The Protection from Redundancy (Pregnancy and Family Leave) Act 2023 further strengthened reg.10 redundancy protections from April 2024. ERA 2025 contains further enacted but uncommenced amendments.

precedingDocuments.title: Pregnant Workers Directive 92/85/EEC
precedingDocuments.significance: Required member states to provide at least 14 weeks' maternity leave with protection from dismissal. The UK implemented 18 weeks OML (later 26) plus AML, exceeding the Directive minimum.

precedingDocuments.title: Parental Leave Directive 96/34/EC
precedingDocuments.significance: Required member states to provide at least 3 months' unpaid parental leave per parent per child. The UK implemented 13 weeks (increased to 18 weeks by SI 2013/283 implementing the revised Directive 2010/18/EU).

precedingDocuments.title: Employment Relations Act 1999
precedingDocuments.significance: Inserted/amended the enabling provisions in ERA 1996 ss.71-80 under which the MPL 1999 Regulations were made. The ERA 1999 was the Blair government's first major employment legislation.

precedingDocuments.title: Protection from Redundancy (Pregnancy and Family Leave) Act 2023
precedingDocuments.significance: Extended enhanced redundancy protection beyond the maternity leave period to include pregnancy and 18 months post-birth. Amended reg.10 and reg.10A of MPL 1999.

precedingDocuments.title: Employment Rights Act 2025
precedingDocuments.significance: Further strengthens redundancy and family leave protections. Several provisions enacted but not yet in force pending commencement orders.

leadingCases.name: Brown v Rentokil Ltd [1998] ECJ Case C-394/96
leadingCases.heldText: Dismissal during pregnancy for pregnancy-related illness is direct sex discrimination throughout the entire pregnancy.
leadingCases.significance: ECJ authority — pregnancy-related illness dismissal is sex discrimination.

leadingCases.name: Sefton BC v Wainwright [2015] UKEAT/0168/14
leadingCases.heldText: The suitable alternative vacancy right in redundancy is a positive offer obligation — the employer must offer without requiring the employee to compete.
leadingCases.significance: Positive offer obligation for maternity redundancy.

leadingCases.name: O'Brien v Bolton St Catherine's Academy [2017] UKSC 16
leadingCases.heldText: Employer cannot select for redundancy because of maternity leave, but no general priority in selection outside reg.10.
leadingCases.significance: Supreme Court authority on maternity leave and redundancy selection.

leadingCases.name: Caledonia Bureau v Caffrey [1998] ICR 603
leadingCases.heldText: Dismissal for pregnancy-related illness is automatically unfair.
leadingCases.significance: Pregnancy-related illness dismissal is automatically unfair under reg.20.

leadingCases.name: GUS Home Shopping v Green [2001] IRLR 75
leadingCases.heldText: Annual leave accrues during maternity leave and cannot be set off against it.

leadingCases.name: Alabaster v Woolwich [2005] ECJ C-147/02
leadingCases.heldText: Pay rises during maternity leave must be reflected in SMP.
leadingCases.significance: SMP must reflect pay rises awarded during maternity leave.

leadingCases.name: Atkins v Coyle Personnel [2008] IRLR 420
leadingCases.heldText: Failure to offer suitable alternative vacancy during maternity redundancy is automatically unfair. The obligation is absolute.
leadingCases.significance: Absolute nature of reg.10 suitable alternative vacancy obligation.
